prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Plasma Physics / AI-Driven Fusion Control.
Below are 8 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
Predict-first TCV and reinforcement-learning plasma-control studies show that data-driven control can learn device-specific dynamics, but ITER-scale deployment needs cross-device transfer, uncertainty-aware safety, and simulator-to-shot adaptation. What fusion-control foundation model would combine differentiable plasma simulators, multi-tokamak logs, and constrained RL to produce robust ramp-up, sustainment, and ramp-down policies across machines?

[Paper 1] Offline Model-Based Reinforcement Learning for Tokamak Control
Brief Abstract: Offline model-based RL for tokamak control: learns a dynamics model from historical DIII-D experimental data and trains an RL policy entirely offline without requiring a physics simulator; successfully tested for both feedforward trajectory design and feedback control on DIII-D, demonstrating that RL can work with offline data alone.

[Paper 2] Avoiding fusion plasma tearing instability with deep reinforcement learning
Brief Abstract: Deep RL agent that actively avoids tearing instabilities — the leading cause of plasma disruptions — on DIII-D tokamak; the agent navigates high-pressure plasma through safe operational paths in real time, maintaining H-mode performance while keeping tearing likelihood below a controllable threshold.

[Paper 3] High-fidelity data-driven dynamics model for reinforcement learning-based control in HL-3 tokamak
Brief Abstract: High-fidelity data-driven dynamics model enables RL-based plasma control on China's HL-3 tokamak; the learned dynamics model serves as a training environment for RL, achieving stable 400-ms, 1-kHz trajectory control with accurate waveform tracking of plasma current and last closed flux surface.

[Paper 4] Learning plasma dynamics and robust rampdown trajectories with predict-first experiments at TCV
Brief Abstract: Neural state-space model (NSSM) combined with RL for robust plasma rampdown trajectory design at TCV; the NSSM learns plasma dynamics from only 311 pulses (5 in high-performance regime), and RL optimizes trajectories that avoid instability limits; predict-first experiments demonstrate 20% plasma current increase from baseline.

[Paper 5] Magnetic control of tokamak plasmas through deep reinforcement learning
Brief Abstract: First deep reinforcement learning system deployed for real-time plasma shape control on a tokamak (TCV at EPFL); the RL agent adjusts 19 magnetic coil voltages 10,000 times per second based on 90 diagnostic measurements, producing diverse plasma configurations including advanced shapes and dual droplets.

[Paper 6] TORAX: A Fast and Differentiable Tokamak Transport Simulator in JAX
Brief Abstract: TORAX: open-source differentiable tokamak transport simulator built in JAX; solves coupled equations for ion/electron heat transport, particle transport, and current diffusion with automatic differentiation, enabling gradient-based optimization of plasma scenarios and seamless integration with ML surrogate models.

[Paper 7] Predicting disruptive instabilities in controlled fusion plasmas through deep learning
Brief Abstract: First deep learning system for cross-machine disruption prediction in tokamak fusion plasmas; trained on DIII-D and JET experimental data, the model reliably forecasts disruptions even on machines it was not trained on — a crucial capability for ITER and future reactors that cannot afford training disruptions.

[Paper 8] Machine learning for disruption warnings on Alcator C-Mod, DIII-D, and EAST
Brief Abstract: Random forest-based disruption warning system deployed in real-time on three tokamaks (Alcator C-Mod, DIII-D, EAST); achieves 80-90% disruption detection rates using only real-time-available plasma parameters, with successful inter-shot and real-time operation — the first ML disruption predictor validated across three distinct machines simultaneously.

Based on the frontier question and the papers above, generate a new research idea.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
{
  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "<specific problem, dataset, population, or phenomenon>",
    "core_method": "<technical method or mechanism, not a vague goal>",
    "limitation_addressed": "<frontier limitation repaired by this idea>",
    "expected_result": "<scientific result or claim if successful>"
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": ["<paper title or source idea used by this proposal>", "..."],
    "reused_parts": ["<specific method, evidence, data, or problem framing reused from the sources>", "..."],
    "changed_parts": ["<specific modified, replaced, combined, or newly added part>", "..."],
    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "<concrete experiment, benchmark, proof, or analysis setup>",
    "baselines": ["<baseline>", "..."],
    "metrics": ["<metric>", "..."],
    "risks": ["<risk>", "..."]
  }
}
